full_response:
Given data  
• Height at which the muon is created  
 d = 30 000 m  
• Proper lifetime we will (naïvely) take as the lifetime seen in the laboratory*  
 τ = 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ s  

(*A “Galilean” calculation assumes no relativistic time–dilation, so the lifetime is the same in every frame.)

The muon must traverse the distance d before it decays:

d = v τ  ⇒  v = d/τ

Compute the speed:

v = 30 000 m / (1.0 × 10⁻⁶ s) = 3.0 × 10¹⁰ m s⁻¹

Expressed as a multiple of the speed of light c (c ≈ 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹):

v / c = 3.0 × 10¹⁰ m s⁻¹ / 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ = 100

Thus the muon would have to travel at one hundred times the speed of light.

\boxed{v = 3.0\times10^{10}\ \text{m/s}\;=\;100\,c\;=\;100\ \text{light-seconds per second}}

Confidence: 92%
